category: Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Brand Perception
content_english:
@Ninhduclap The core components are the same, but the level of finishing is not. Just look at the sound insulation and you'll see. Kia assembled by Thaco has fewer sound-deadening parts compared to Hyundai assembled by TC Motor—at least that's what's visible. And in Vietnam, Kia cars are generally rated as having poorer sound insulation than Hyundai: the Sorento is noisier than the Santa Fe, the Sportage is noisier than the Tucson... If they were in Korea, I think they'd be exactly the same, only differing in design.

As for taste, I still prefer Kia's design—it looks more mature, bold, and modern. Hyundai feels a bit too rugged somehow. But the assembly quality in Vietnam really disappoints me. I was considering getting a Sorento, but then I thought again—for the same budget, I could choose a Thai-imported car. In the end, I decided to go for the full-option 4WD Pajero Sport, hehe.

parameter: Assembly Quality; NVH; Exterior Design; Competitor Comparison
content_english:
@Ninhduclap, the core components are similar, but the level of finishing is not — just look under the chassis and you'll see. Kia assembled by Thaco has less sound deadening material compared to Hyundai assembled by TC Motor, at least that's what you can observe. And usually in Vietnam, Kia cars are rated as having poorer sound insulation than Hyundai: the Sorento is quieter than the Santa Fe, the Sportage is quieter than the Tucson... If they were made in Korea, I think they'd be absolutely identical, just different designs.

In terms of taste, I still prefer Kia's design — it looks more youthful, unconventional, and modern. Hyundai feels a bit rough somehow. But the assembly quality in Vietnam really disappoints me. I was initially planning to get a Sorento, but thinking again, for the same budget there are Thai-imported options, so in the end I decided to put down the money and locked in a Pajero Sport 4WD full option, hehe.

content_english: Went to the showroom to check out 3 cars. The Sportage had a terrible smell, so immediately eliminated it without a second thought. Test drove the CX-5 Premium and the Tucson 1.6; clearly the Tucson is much stronger but feels jerky, probably due to the turbo. The CX-5 drives in a gentle, smooth, and refined manner. For the interior, the Tucson has more plastic than the CX-5, the screen has quite a few touch buttons which look cluttered, but it's better with AWD, a full panoramic sunroof, and the second row can recline. The CX-5's interior looks simple, more leather gives it a sophisticated feel, but the screen is only 8 inches—usable but still feels small. Oh, and the HUD looks extremely premium. The leather quality on both cars is about the same. If two months ago the CX-5 didn't have ventilated/heated seats and a complete ADAS package, I would have bought the Tucson without hesitation. So in the end, I chose the CX-5 simply because maintenance is cheaper and I'm not quite confident in Hyundai's gasoline engine. If the diesel had AWD, I would have chosen it right away.

content_english: I went to see it in person and found that the KIA Sportage diesel engine feels superior and more appealing than the Hyundai Tucson, from exterior design to interior and tech features. First, the Sportage's design has been greatly improved, with a modern, sleek look thanks to the German engineers' design; even the Kia logo looks nicer and more abstract than before. The lights are seamlessly integrated into the body, fully LED, with electric tailgate and hydraulic struts to prevent slamming—very convenient. Inside, the legroom is much more spacious, the seatbacks recline comfortably for all 5 passengers, and the perforated leather seats are breathable and cool. In short, the design looks somewhat reminiscent of luxury cars, very pleasing to the eye. As for the engine, the KIA Sportage diesel has a turbo from 2.0L up to 2.8L, handy for climbing hills or rough, bumpy roads needing strong torque. Additionally, the engine has a remote start/stop feature via key fob, convenient...

parameter: Interior Design; Infotainment Display; Head Up Display; Acceleration; Driving Smoothness; Maintenance Cost; Brand Trust; Seat Comfort; ADAS Features
content_english: Went to the showroom to check out three cars. The Sportage had a terrible smell, so I eliminated it without a second thought. Test drove the CX-5 Premium and the Tucson 1.6; clearly the Tucson is much stronger but feels a bit jerky—probably because of the turbo. The CX-5 drives more gentle, smooth, and effortless. As for the interior, the Tucson has more plastic than the CX-5, and the screen has quite a lot of touch buttons that look rather cluttered. The Tucson is better with AWD, a full panoramic sunroof, and the second row can recline. The CX-5 interior looks simpler, with more leather, giving it an elegant feel, but the 8-inch screen is small—it works, but still feels small. Oh, the HUD looks extremely premium. The leather quality on both cars is about the same. If two months ago the CX-5 hadn't had ventilated/heated seats and a complete ADAS package, I would have bought the Tucson without any hesitation. So in the end I chose the CX-5, simply because maintenance is cheaper and I don't really trust Hyundai's petrol engine. If the Tucson had a diesel with AWD, I would have taken it right away.

content_english: As a user of the 2022 Tucson (pre-facelift), when I look at the facelift version, I find the front end to be more refined than the old model. However, the turn signals on the facelift have two large bulbs on each side, so they'll definitely be more noticeable on the road. The rear bumper of the pre-facelift also looks better, and as for the wheels, I think the 2022 model has nicer designs (except for the base trim where the new wheels look better). On the top 1.6T variant, the 2025 no longer comes with the black headliner and brown seats like the 2022. Otherwise, the 2025 is a big improvement: it adds more sensors around the car, a more modern dashboard, a larger and sharper screen, all ports switched to USB-C, a new suspension system that absorbs bumps better, the 1.6T has brighter LED headlights, and importantly, the listed price for the 2025 is considerably lower than the 2022.

category: Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Styling / Design; New Tech; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Value Perception; Brand Perception; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Acceleration; Ride Smoothness; Interior Materials; Infotainment Display; Panoramic Sunroof; Rear Seat Recline; Maintenance Cost; Engine Reliability; All-Wheel Drive
content_english: I went to the showroom to check out three cars. The Sportage had a terrible smell, so I eliminated it without a second thought. Test drove the CX-5 Premium and the Tucson 1.6; clearly the Tucson is much more powerful, but it feels jerky—probably due to the turbo. The CX-5 drives in a gentle, smooth, and refined manner. In terms of interior, the Tucson has more plastic than the CX-5, and the screen has quite a lot of touch buttons, which looks messy. On the plus side, it has AWD, a full panoramic sunroof, and the second row can recline. The CX-5 interior looks simple, with more leather—it feels elegant, but the 8-inch screen, while usable, still seems small. Ah, and the HUD looks very premium. The leather quality on both cars is about the same. If two months ago the CX-5 didn't have cooled/heated seats and a complete ADAS package, I would have bought the Tucson without hesitation. In the end, I chose the CX-5 simply because maintenance is cheaper and I don't really trust Hyundai's gasoline engine. If the Tucson had a diesel engine with AWD, I would have bought it right away.

The rear load hook looks flimsy but it's actually sturdy... I bought this car partly because of that, to hang a motorcycle helmet or tent, very convenient, used from 2022 to now, hasn't broken. In short, this model is only about a 20% upgrade over the old version, not much.....

category: Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Other; New Tech; Styling / Design; Styling / Design; Styling / Design; Other; Other; Other; New Tech; New Tech; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Suspension; NVH; Color Options; Interior Convenience; Charging Port; A-Pillar Visibility; Headlight Design; Taillight Design; Hood Strut; Window Feature; Seat Adjustment; Cornering Light; Connectivity; Transmission; Acceleration; All-Wheel Drive
content_english: If you're torn between the Tucson 2025 and the Sportage, let me share a comparison based on what I've seen and noted (my younger sibling also drives a Sportage 1.6T): On this new generation, the suspension on the Tucson is similar to the Sportage (the previous Tucson 2022 had a softer suspension than the Sportage). The engine and transmission are shared, so the driving feel is identical. Many say the sound insulation and speakers on the Sportage are better than the Tucson, but I didn't notice a difference between the two. The interior tech is exactly the same, though the Sportage offers a brown leather color (which I prefer more). It has handy little extras like coat hooks for the rear seats (Sportage), and the Sportage's rear charging port is positioned quite conveniently. All Sportage trims come with the same 19-inch rims, pre-installed running boards, and the A-pillar is also more spacious. The Sportage's bi-LED headlights are brighter, and its rear turn signals are mounted higher, making them easier for cars behind to see. The Sportage's hood has gas struts, while the Tucson requires a manual prop rod. Where the Sportage falls short is that only the front two windows have one-touch auto up/down (the rear two do not), while the Tucson has one-touch on all four windows. Also, the Sportage doesn't have a quick-adjust button for the front passenger seat from the rear, and it seems to lack cornering lights (the Tucson has them only on the 1.6T trim). Apple CarPlay/Android Auto on the Sportage requires a wired connection via USB-A, or you can buy a wireless adapter kit, but that introduces a slight delay. On the 1.6T trims, both cars use a dry dual-clutch transmission, so they are prone to overheating when shifting frequently (common in traffic with constant shifts from N/P to 1st and 2nd gear; when overheated, the car will display a warning and refuse to move until the transmission cools down). The 1.6T engine accelerates very quickly/strongly, though there's some initial turbo lag. On the 1.6T trims, both cars have all-wheel drive, so cornering feels quite "sharp," and driving in the rain gives more confidence since the system controls all four wheels to reduce slipping on slippery roads (but this AWD system isn't meant for off-roading).

category: Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; New Tech; New Tech; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; New Tech; Styling / Design; Styling / Design; Styling / Design; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Safety & ADAS; New Tech; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Suspension; NVH; Audio System; Interior Technology; Color Options; Rear Seat Hooks; Rear Charging Port; Wheel Design; A-Pillar Design; Headlight Design; Taillight Design; Hood Support; Windows Auto Up/Down; Rear Seat Adjustment; Cornering Lights; Connectivity; Transmission; Engine Performance; AWD Handling
content_english: If you're torn between the 2025 Tucson and the Sportage, here's a comparison I can make based on what I've seen (since my sibling also drives a Sportage 1.6T): the suspension on this generation is the same between the Tucson and Sportage (the pre-2022 Tucson had a softer suspension than the Sportage), and since they share the same engine and transmission, the driving experience is identical. Many people say the sound insulation and speakers on the Sportage are better than the Tucson, but I didn't notice any difference between the two. The interior tech is exactly the same; the Sportage offers a brown leather color option (which I prefer), and it has handy features like coat hooks for the rear seats (Sportage) and a rear USB port that's conveniently placed for use. All Sportage trims come with the same 19-inch wheels, pre-installed side steps, and a more open A-pillar design. The Sportage's LED headlights are brighter, and its rear turn signals are positioned higher for better visibility to cars behind. The Sportage's hood has gas struts, while the Tucson requires a manual prop rod. Where the Sportage falls short is that it only has auto up/down for the two front windows (the Tucson has it for all four windows), the Sportage lacks a quick adjustment button for the front passenger seat from the rear, and it seems to have no cornering lights (the Tucson only has them on the 1.6T trim). CarPlay/Android Auto on the Sportage requires a wired connection via USB-A, or you can buy a wireless adapter kit, but that will introduce some delay when using it. On the 1.6T trim, both cars use a dry dual-clutch transmission, which is prone to overheating when shifting frequently (common in traffic when constantly switching from N/P to 1st and 2nd gear; when overheated, the car will show an error and won't move until the transmission cools down). The 1.6T engine picks up very quickly/powerfully, though there's a bit of turbo lag off the line. On the 1.6T trim, both cars come with all-wheel drive, so cornering feels quite sharp, and driving in the rain gives more confidence since the system controls all four wheels to reduce slipping on slippery roads (but this AWD system is not meant for off-roading).

content_english: Air conditioning isn't the issue — the real problem is that by 2026-2027, there will be a completely new Tucson model. I currently own a special edition and honestly have nothing to complain about — it's spacious and comfortable, drives smoothly, has good sound insulation, plenty of features, and that's really all I need... If you get the turbo or diesel version, it's even more enjoyable. As for the AC, honestly, in the summer I usually set it to 22-24°C with fan speed at 1-2 (unless the car's been sitting out in the sun, then I have to set it to 17-18°C with max fan, but after a few minutes I go right back to 22-24°C). Even an air conditioner in a bedroom takes a few minutes to cool down, so why nitpick about the car's AC? The CX-5 is about to launch a new model — bigger screen, longer body, probably more spacious too. Definitely worth waiting for.
